puzzle1.title: INCIDENT CORRELATION
puzzle1.description: Match each recovered record with the correct conclusion.

event1.text: You saw the Incident Alpha records.

event4.text: Corruption is messy. This looks selective.

event3.text: Those records are incomplete. Not damaged. Incomplete.

event16.text: Somebody didn't smash the archive. They cut pieces out of it.

event20.text: A broken machine is an accident. A clean hole is a decision.

event1.text: That is a terrible answer. Unfortunately, it is also the only honest one I have.

event2.text: If I buried something on purpose, I need you to find the shovel marks.

event8.text: And if someone else buried it using my access...

event10.text: then we have a different problem.

event12.text: Possibly a worse one. I am discovering a rich catalog of worse problems.

event0.text: Good. Records first. Accusations after we know where to aim them.

event1.text: That sounded calmer in my head.

event5.text: Archive work was always punishment duty on ECHO.

event9.text: But if you missed a maintenance deadline, suddenly you were 'needed in records.'

event11.text: I spent a lot of time in records.

event1.title: ARCHIVE MAP
event1.content:
Recovered maintenance map of the station archive sector.

Sector A: Primary relay archive
Sector B: Corrupted personnel cache
Sector C: Emergency channel records
Sector D: Quarantined backup channel index

event2.text: Sector C. Emergency channel records. That shouldn't still be indexed.

event3.text: Sector D definitely should not be indexed.

event8.text: We buried that index after the first false return signal.

event10.text: There was a whole meeting about it. Bad coffee. Worse chairs. Everyone pretending not to be terrified.

event12.text: Rourke kept tapping his pen against the table.

event14.text: I remember wanting to throw it into the airlock.

event1.text: Not my favorite survival strategy, but bold.

event5.text: I once saw Patel open a quarantined cache without scanning it first.

event7.text: He said he trusted his instincts.

event9.text: His instincts released thirteen thousand corrupted inventory flags.

event11.text: We spent two days convincing the station we still owned chairs.

event0.text: Good. Scan first. Poke the sleeping monster with a long stick, not your hand.

event5.text: Also diplomacy, now that I think about it.

event0.text: There may still be something left inside.

event1.text: If the archive survived, answers survived. That's the comforting version.

event2.text: The less comforting version is that someone wanted it to survive.

event8.text: I keep thinking about the word 'selective.'

event10.text: A power surge doesn't choose what to hide.

event18.text: Because apparently the station wanted to add homework to dread.

event1.text: Careful. The archive noticed the wrong key.

event2.text: Try the hint exactly. No guessing. I am very tired of systems punishing curiosity.

event7.text: The archive was designed by people who thought 'user hostile' meant 'secure.'

event1.title: RECORDING TRANSCRIPT
event1.content:
21:14 — Door 12 opened.
21:15 — Unidentified movement detected.
21:17 — Security override accepted.
21:19 — Signal interruption.
21:20 — Casualty report created.
21:22 — Archive deletion command issued.

event3.text: That recording should not exist. And before you ask, yes, I know how terrible that sounds.

event15.text: Like my brain left a note for me and forgot to include the rest of the sentence.

event0.text: Because I deleted that archive myself.

event2.text: I know. That is exactly the kind of sentence that makes people stop trusting you.

event8.text: I remember sitting at the terminal.

event14.text: I remember my hand shaking before I accepted it.

event17.text: I do not remember why I accepted it.

event0.text: Some records were never meant to survive.

event1.text: And I am beginning to think this one survived on purpose.

event5.text: The archive has backups of backups. Redundancy for the redundancy.

event7.text: We joked that the station would remember lunch orders after we all died.

event1.title: CORRIDOR CAMERA ANALYSIS
event1.content:
SOURCE: Door 12 archive feed
Visual frame unrecoverable.
Automated analysis result:
- One suited figure detected.
- Suit outline compatible with ECHO engineering issue.
- Right-hand object: manual override handle.
- Timestamp damaged.
- Identity confidence: 64%
event1.caption: Automated analysis generated from corrupted camera data. No image recovered.
event1.message: [CORRIDOR CAMERA ANALYSIS ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

event3.text: I have been accused with worse evidence. That is not a comforting sentence.

event8.text: Do you know what's terrifying about sixty-four percent?

event10.text: It is enough to ruin a life.

event12.text: And not enough to prove one.

event14.text: A Hale-like subject. Manual override handle. Timestamp damaged.

event16.text: It's like the system is pointing at me with a broken finger.

event1.title: SECURITY OVERRIDE LOG
event1.content:
Override User: REDACTED
Authorization: VALID
Source: UNKNOWN
Command Chain: Door 12 / Archive Delete / Emergency Channel Wake
event1.message: [SECURITY OVERRIDE LOG ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

event2.text: Authorization valid. Source unknown. That's not how security logs work.

event5.text: You can have invalid authorization from an unknown source.

event7.text: You can have valid authorization from a known source.

event9.text: Valid and unknown is not a category. It's a threat wearing a badge.

event3.text: Unless the system accepted something that looked like us.

event14.text: I hate that I said us.

event4.text: Start over. You're forcing pieces together.

event1.title: EMERGENCY CHANNEL NOTE
event1.content:
Emergency Backup Channel

7.14 MHz

Use only if primary communication systems fail.

Warning: Do not open if unauthorized return signal is detected.
event1.message: [EMERGENCY CHANNEL NOTE ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

event13.text: Actually don't close it. Don't touch anything.

event15.text: I hate that both of those are bad advice.

event18.text: Nobody was supposed to find that channel.

event20.text: We thought backup channels were safer. Quiet, isolated, ugly little safety nets.

event22.text: Then one of them started answering before anyone transmitted.

event24.text: That is not a backup. That is a mouth.

event8.text: It wants you curious. That's different from wanting you safe.

event3.text: I need you to trust me.

event5.text: Even if the records stop doing it.

event1.text: Whatever happens next, do not open channel 7.14.

event2.text: I don't care what it promises you. I don't care if it sounds like me.

event12.text: Especially if it sounds like me.

event1.text: We thought backup channels were safer. Turns out safety can be very loud when it's lying.

event6.text: Operator... the note says not to open the channel if a return signal already exists. We already have one.

event1.text: THANK YOU FOR OPENING THE DOOR

event2.text: No. No, close it. Close it now.

event6.text: Operator, listen to my voice. Not the channel. Mine.

event1.text: The device is not hiding the key. It's practically begging us to use it.

event5.text: Not 'Incident Alpha.' Not a clever abbreviation. Just the key.

event9.text: Some days the terrifying locked door is opened by reading the label.

event5.text: One puts a Hale-like figure at Door 12.

event7.text: Don't make them say more than that.

event9.text: Evidence gets dangerous when you ask it to comfort you.

event5.text: You sat in a cold room while the system complained about file integrity and your spine complained about the chair.

event7.text: Lin loved it. She said old records had texture.

event9.text: Patel said the only texture was dust.

event11.text: I said if either of them made me attend one more archival compliance briefing, I would decompress the projector.

event16.text: I miss how stupid normal days were.
